content.text: Packaging an agent application for container deployment requires more than copying files into a Dockerfile. Stateless processes, configurable networking, graceful shutdown on SIGTERM, health probes, and externalized state are the foundations that allow orchestrators like Kubernetes and ECS to scale, replace, and update agent containers without data loss or orphaned side effects.

Containerizing an agent application means packaging every runtime dependency — the Python interpreter, libraries, LLM client, and application code — into a self-contained OCI image. When that image runs as a container it becomes a short-lived, replaceable process whose only durable state is stored outside the container: in a database, cache, or object-storage bucket.
For agentic workloads the packaging decision is more nuanced than a simple web app. Agents often span two distinct process roles: a synchronous
that accepts requests and enqueues work, and an asynchronous

that executes multi-step tool loops. These roles have different resource profiles, scaling axes, and restart-safety requirements, so they typically live in separate images (or at minimum separate container definitions).
A well-containerized agent service obeys the
principles: configuration from environment variables, backing services treated as attached resources, processes that start fast, exit cleanly, and never store mutable state on local disk.

Container orchestrators replace unhealthy instances automatically. If an API container crashes, the orchestrator starts a fresh one within seconds. If the worker is mid-run when it receives SIGTERM for a rolling deploy, it must either finish gracefully or requeue the task — otherwise tool side effects (database writes, emails sent, files created) are left half-applied with no automatic recovery.

Scalability also depends on statelessness. An API pod that stores run state in an in-process Python dict breaks the moment a second replica starts — the next request may land on a different pod with no knowledge of the prior conversation. Every piece of mutable state must live in a shared external store from day one.

Container images enable environment parity: the exact same image that passes CI integration tests is promoted to staging and then production, with only environment variables changing between environments. This eliminates entire classes of 'works on my machine' bugs and makes deployments auditable.
— run N API replicas and M worker replicas from the same image; add replicas in seconds when queue depth rises.
— each release is a new image tag; rollback is a tag swap, not a code revert.
— CPU and memory limits per container prevent a runaway LLM loop from exhausting the host and impacting other services.
— dev, staging, and prod all run the same image; only environment variables differ.
— a stateless container reaches ready state in seconds because all warm-up is in-process, not from local disk state.
— the Python version, C extensions, and library versions are baked into the image layer and never conflict with the host OS or other services.
— separate API and worker Dockerfiles mean two image builds, two vulnerability scans, and coordinated versioning.
— long LLM tool loops (potentially minutes) must either complete before the container exits or implement checkpoint-and-requeue logic.
— Docker Compose volumes work locally but do not substitute for managed Redis or Postgres in production; local runs can mask connection bugs.
Layer cache misses on large deps
— re-ordering COPY instructions so requirements install before source code is critical, or builds become slow on every change.
— environment variables must be injected securely via an orchestrator secret store; baking secrets into images is a critical anti-pattern.
A Dockerfile defines a sequence of layers. Docker (and compatible builders) cache each layer keyed by its inputs. The most impactful ordering rule: copy and install dependencies
copying application source. This way a source-only change skips the slow pip install layer and reuses the cache, cutting build times from minutes to seconds for common edits.
Multi-stage builds reduce final image size. A
stage compiles wheels or downloads model artifacts; a
stage copies only the compiled output into a slim base image. The intermediate build tools (gcc, pip, build headers) never land in the shipped image, reducing attack surface and pull latency.
Each container should run exactly one process (PID 1). For FastAPI, Uvicorn manages one or more async worker threads. For Celery, the worker process is PID 1. Using an init system (the
Docker flag or Tini) ensures SIGTERM propagates correctly to child processes and zombie processes are reaped before container exit.

Every deployment-time value — database URL, Redis URL, API keys, log level, concurrency count — is read from environment variables. The image contains no secrets. Kubernetes Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager, or HashiCorp Vault inject values at runtime. Pydantic Settings (pydantic-settings) is the idiomatic Python library for this pattern, providing typed access with validation on startup.
Orchestrators send two kinds of probes. A
probe restarts a container that is deadlocked: it returns 200 as long as the event loop is processing requests. A

probe gates traffic: it returns 200 only when upstream dependencies (Redis, Postgres) are reachable. Separating them prevents the orchestrator from routing traffic to a container whose connection pool is not yet warmed up after startup.

When the orchestrator wants to stop a container it sends SIGTERM and then waits a configurable termination period (default 30 s in Kubernetes) before sending SIGKILL. FastAPI lifespan context managers run cleanup on SIGTERM; Celery worker signals allow in-flight tasks to finish or requeue. Both patterns must be implemented for a safe rolling deploy.

The diagram below shows a typical two-tier agent deployment: stateless API replicas behind a load balancer, asynchronous worker replicas pulling from a Redis task queue, and shared Postgres plus object storage as external state. All configuration flows in through environment variables from the orchestrator.

attrs.alt: Agentic-AI container architecture: API replicas, worker replicas, Redis queue, Postgres, object storage, image registry

content.text: Key structural points: API and worker containers share no in-process state. They communicate through Redis (task queue) and Postgres (run records). The image registry stores versioned tags that the orchestrator pulls on deploy. Object storage (S3/GCS) holds uploaded documents; ephemeral container disk is never a dependency for durability.

Use separate Dockerfiles when the API and worker have meaningfully different base images (e.g., worker needs CUDA), different scale profiles (20 API pods vs 5 GPU worker pods), or different resource limits. This is the standard recommendation for any production agent deployment expected to scale.
Acceptable for small projects or early prototypes: one Dockerfile, one image tag, but the orchestrator overrides the CMD at deploy time (
vs the default Uvicorn CMD). Both API and worker share the same layer cache and image tag. The downside is that a GPU dependency added for workers bloats the API image unnecessarily.

For throwaway scripts, local notebooks, or one-off batch jobs that run once and produce a file, container overhead adds no value. Containers pay off when the workload needs to be scheduled, scaled, monitored, updated, and rolled back reliably in a shared infrastructure.
The following files form a production-grade containerization of a FastAPI agent server and its Celery worker. Each file is complete and deployable.

# Dockerfile  (API)
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.lock .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.lock

FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
# Copy only compiled packages, not build tools
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY src/ src/

# Non-root user for security
RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' agent
USER agent

ENV PORT=8000
EXPOSE 8000

HEALTHCHECK --interval=15s --timeout=5s --retries=3 \
  CMD python -c "import urllib.request; \
    urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8000/health')"

CMD ["python", "-m", "uvicorn", "src.api.main:app",
     "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000", "--workers", "2"]

# Dockerfile.worker
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.lock .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.lock

FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY src/ src/

RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' agent
USER agent

# Worker concurrency and queue from env; defaults to 4 concurrent tasks
ENV CELERY_CONCURRENCY=4
ENV CELERY_QUEUES=agent

CMD ["sh", "-c",
     "celery -A src.worker.app worker -Q $CELERY_QUEUES -c $CELERY_CONCURRENCY --loglevel=info"]
Step 3 — FastAPI app with lifespan, health, and readiness

# src/api/main.py
import os
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

import httpx
import redis.asyncio as aioredis
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker

DATABASE_URL = os.environ["DATABASE_URL"]
REDIS_URL = os.environ["REDIS_URL"]

engine = create_async_engine(DATABASE_URL, pool_size=5, max_overflow=10)
AsyncSessionLocal = sessionmaker(engine, class_=AsyncSession, expire_on_commit=False)
redis_client: aioredis.Redis | None = None
http_client: httpx.AsyncClient | None = None


@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    global redis_client, http_client
    redis_client = aioredis.from_url(REDIS_URL, decode_responses=True)
    http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30)
    yield
    # Graceful shutdown: close connections before container exits
    await redis_client.aclose()
    await http_client.aclose()
    await engine.dispose()


app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)


@app.get('/health')
async def health():
    # Liveness probe: always 200 if the event loop is running.
    return {'status': 'ok'}


@app.get('/ready')
async def ready():
    # Readiness probe: 200 only when backing services are reachable.
    errors = []
    try:
        await redis_client.ping()
    except Exception as exc:
        errors.append(f"redis: {exc}")
    try:
        async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
            await session.execute("SELECT 1")
    except Exception as exc:
        errors.append(f"db: {exc}")
    if errors:
        return {"status": "unavailable", "errors": errors}, 503
    return {"status": "ready"}

# docker-compose.yml
services:
  api:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    env_file: .env
    depends_on:
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

  worker:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.worker
    env_file: .env
    environment:
      CELERY_CONCURRENCY: "2"   # lower than prod for local
    depends_on:
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: agentdb
      POSTGRES_USER: agent
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: local_only
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U agent -d agentdb"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
Example 1 — Celery worker with graceful SIGTERM drain
This example shows the Celery configuration options and signal hooks that allow an in-flight tool loop to finish when the container receives a stop signal, rather than being killed mid-step.

# src/worker/app.py
import os
import logging

from celery import Celery
from celery.signals import worker_shutdown

REDIS_URL = os.environ["REDIS_URL"]

app = Celery("agent_worker", broker=REDIS_URL, backend=REDIS_URL)

app.conf.update(
    task_acks_late=True,            # ack only after task completes
    task_reject_on_worker_lost=True, # requeue if worker dies mid-task
    worker_prefetch_multiplier=1,   # one task at a time per thread
    task_time_limit=600,            # hard kill after 10 min
    task_soft_time_limit=540,       # SoftTimeLimitExceeded at 9 min
)

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@worker_shutdown.connect
def on_worker_shutdown(sender, **kwargs):
    logger.info("Worker shutdown signal received — draining in-flight tasks")


@app.task(bind=True, max_retries=3, default_retry_delay=30)
def run_agent_task(self, run_id: str, user_message: str):
    # Execute one agent turn.
    # task_acks_late + task_reject_on_worker_lost ensure requeue on kill.
    from src.agent.runner import AgentRunner
    try:
        runner = AgentRunner(run_id=run_id)
        result = runner.run(user_message)
        return result
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.exception("Agent task %s failed, retrying", run_id)
        raise self.retry(exc=exc)
Centralizing environment variable parsing with Pydantic Settings gives typed access, default values, startup validation, and clear documentation of every required and optional variable in the image.

# src/config.py
from pydantic import AnyUrl, Field
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_file=".env", env_file_encoding="utf-8")

    # Backing services — required
    database_url: AnyUrl = Field(..., description='Async-compatible PostgreSQL URL')
    redis_url: AnyUrl = Field(..., description='Redis URL for task broker and cache')

    # Secrets — required
    openai_api_key: str = Field(..., description='LLM provider key')

    # Optional tuning
    log_level: str = Field('INFO', description='Python logging level')
    worker_concurrency: int = Field(4, description='Celery worker concurrency')
    api_workers: int = Field(2, description='Uvicorn worker count')
    port: int = Field(8000, description='HTTP listen port')
    environment: str = Field('production', description='dev | staging | production')


settings = Settings()

# Usage in any module:
# from src.config import settings
# engine = create_async_engine(str(settings.database_url))
A document-analysis agent platform serves legal teams. The deployment uses two image families built from a monorepo:
. Both image tags are bumped together on every merge to main via GitHub Actions.

image runs Uvicorn with two async worker processes behind an Application Load Balancer. It handles document ingestion (POST /documents), job submission (POST /runs), and status polling (GET /runs/{id}). All run records and extracted text are written to Aurora Postgres via SQLAlchemy async sessions. The API container never touches local disk.

image runs Celery with eight concurrent threads on compute-optimized instances. Workers pull from an SQS-backed Celery broker. Each task executes a multi-step Claude tool loop, streaming intermediate results to a Postgres JSONB column. If a worker pod is evicted mid-task, task_acks_late ensures the task is requeued and the next available worker picks it up transparently.

Uploaded PDF documents are streamed to S3 immediately on receipt; the API container holds them only in memory long enough to stream them to the bucket. Rolling deploys use maxUnavailable: 1 and maxSurge: 1 so there is always at least one API pod and one worker pod running during the transition.
Storing state in the container process

The most common mistake is an in-memory data structure — a Python dict, a module-level list — that accumulates run state. This works with one replica but breaks silently when a second API pod starts, because requests fan across pods and neither has the full picture. Every run record must land in Postgres before the response is returned.

Configuring only a liveness probe, or pointing both probes at the same endpoint, causes two problems. If the liveness probe checks Redis and Redis is momentarily slow, the orchestrator restarts a healthy container unnecessarily. If there is no readiness probe, traffic hits the container before its connection pool is ready, causing an error burst on every deploy.
Writing logs to the container filesystem

Logs written to a file inside the container vanish when the container restarts. Log aggregation systems consume stdout/stderr from the container runtime. Configure Python logging to stream to stdout only.

# src/logging_setup.py
import logging
import sys


def configure_logging(level: str = 'INFO') -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(
        stream=sys.stdout,         # orchestrator captures stdout
        level=getattr(logging, level.upper(), logging.INFO),
        format='%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s %(message)s',
    )

or secrets in COPY'd config files exposes credentials to anyone who can pull the image. Always inject secrets at runtime via Kubernetes Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager, or equivalent — never bake them into image layers.
Order Dockerfile layers by change frequency
— install dependencies before copying source code; source changes will not invalidate the dependency cache.
— a builder stage compiles wheels; the runtime stage copies only compiled artifacts into a slim image, typically cutting size 50–80 %.
— add an unprivileged user and set USER before CMD; this limits the blast radius of any container escape.
— liveness (event loop alive) and readiness (backing services reachable) must be separate endpoints.
Set CPU and memory limits on every container
— unbounded LLM loops can exhaust node memory; resource limits prevent noisy-neighbor failures.
— defer the broker acknowledgement until the task finishes so a killed worker causes requeue, not silent loss.
Pin base image digests in CI
which can silently pull breaking patches.
file with every key, a description, and a safe placeholder value.
Container performance for agent services is shaped by three independent dimensions: image pull latency, startup time, and steady-state throughput.

A 4 GB image with bundled model weights adds 60–120 seconds to cold-start time. Strategies: keep model weights out of the image (download from S3 on first use or use an init container), use layer caching at the registry (ECR, GHCR), and prefer slim base images. A typical Python API image should be under 500 MB; a worker with ML deps under 2 GB.

FastAPI with Pydantic v2 starts in under one second. The bottleneck is usually connection pool warm-up. Pre-connect by pinging Redis and Postgres in the lifespan startup block before the readiness probe returns 200. Uvicorn's --workers flag forks after startup, so warm-up runs once in the parent process rather than once per worker.

API replicas scale horizontally with no coordination. Worker replicas scale based on queue depth: when the Celery queue length exceeds a threshold, KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) or a custom HPA metric adds worker pods. LLM-bound tasks are heavily I/O-bound (waiting on API responses), so worker concurrency well above the CPU core count is typical — eight concurrent tasks per two-core pod is a common starting point.

# kubernetes/worker-deployment.yaml (resource section)
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "1Gi"
  limits:
    cpu: "2000m"
    memory: "4Gi"   # safeguard against large context windows

# KEDA ScaledObject — scale workers on Redis queue depth
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-scaledobject
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: agent-worker
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  triggers:
  - type: redis
    metadata:
      address: redis:6379
      listName: celery
      listLength: "10"   # one pod per 10 queued tasks
— standard local development runtime; buildx plugin for multi-platform (amd64/arm64) builds.
— rootless, daemonless alternative to Docker; drop-in CLI compatible, preferred in environments where root-level daemons are restricted.
— default Docker build backend; parallel layer builds, secrets mount (
— native integration with ECS and EKS; lifecycle policies prune old tags automatically.
— built into GitHub Actions; free for public images, straightforward authentication with GITHUB_TOKEN.
— successor to GCR; integrates with Cloud Run and GKE, supports vulnerability scanning.
— local development and CI smoke tests; not for production scale.
— production-grade; Deployments, HPA, KEDA for queue-based worker autoscaling, NetworkPolicy for isolation.
— managed containers without node management; Task Definitions map closely to Docker Compose service definitions.
— serverless containers on GCP; cold starts are the main constraint for latency-sensitive agent APIs.

# .github/workflows/build-push.yml (abbreviated)
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
      - uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          file: Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/agent-api:${{ github.sha }}
          cache-from: type=registry,ref=ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/agent-api:cache
          cache-to: type=registry,ref=ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/agent-api:cache,mode=max

Every base image layer is a potential attack surface. Use distroless or slim base images to minimize installed packages. Run vulnerability scans (Trivy, Grype, Snyk) in CI and block image promotion on critical CVEs. Pin base image digests so supply-chain attacks cannot silently introduce new packages between builds.

API keys and database credentials must never be baked into an image. Inject them as environment variables from Kubernetes Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager (with the ECS Secrets integration), or GCP Secret Manager. Audit access to secret stores independently from image access — different IAM roles should control who can pull the image vs. who can read the database password.

In Kubernetes, use NetworkPolicy to restrict which pods can reach which services. The worker needs Postgres and Redis; the API needs Postgres, Redis, and the LLM provider endpoint. Nothing else requires cross-pod connectivity. In ECS, use task-level security groups to enforce the same boundaries. Default-deny network policies are the safest baseline.
— the orchestrator waits for the new pod's /ready endpoint before shifting traffic; misconfigured probes are the most common cause of deployment-induced outages.
— prevent the cluster from evicting all API pods simultaneously during a node drain; set minAvailable: 1 for both API and worker deployments.
— because tasks can be retried, every agent task must be safe to run twice; use the run_id as an idempotency key when writing results to avoid duplicate records.
— wrap the LLM client with tenacity or equivalent retry logic with exponential backoff; set a maximum retry budget to prevent a thundering herd during provider outages.
Containers enforce statelessness: all mutable run state lives in Postgres or Redis, making replicas interchangeable and replacements safe without data loss.

Separate the API container (synchronous, low-memory, stateless) from the worker container (async, LLM-bound, high-concurrency); they scale on different axes and benefit from independent resource limits.
Every deployment-time value — database URL, API keys, concurrency counts — comes from environment variables; secrets are injected at runtime by the orchestrator, never baked into the image.
Implement both a liveness probe (/health, always 200) and a readiness probe (/ready, checks Redis + DB reachability); confusing the two causes unnecessary restarts or premature traffic routing.
Handle SIGTERM in FastAPI lifespan hooks and configure Celery with task_acks_late + task_reject_on_worker_lost so in-flight agent tool loops finish or requeue rather than being killed mid-step.
Order Dockerfile layers by change frequency (dependencies before source) and use multi-stage builds to keep final image sizes small and build caches effective across rapid iteration cycles.
Run containers as a non-root user, scan images for CVEs in CI, and use NetworkPolicy or security groups to restrict lateral movement between services.
Object storage (S3/GCS) for uploaded documents and stdout for logs are non-negotiable in a containerized agent service; ephemeral container disk must never be a dependency for durability.
